title: Huntrex 277 (Pty) Ltd v Margo Berzak and Others
text:
APPLICANT’S COUNSEL DETAILS  APPLICANT’S ATTORNEYS DETAILS 
Adv S Rosenberg SC 
Email.: rosenberg@capebar.co.za 
Mobile.: 072 831 1554 
Adv TR Tyler 
Email.: ttyler@law.co.za 
Mobile.: 082 709 6344 
 Mr SG Lamprecht 
LAMPRECHT & ASSOCIATES INC 
Email.: stephen@lamprecht-attorneys.co.za 
Mobile.: 074 969 6437  
IN THE CONSTITUTIONAL COURT OF SOUTH AFRICA 
 CCT case no : 69/2023 
SCA case no.: 210/2021 
WCC case no.: 6407/2020  
In the matter between:   
HUNTREX 277 (PTY) LTD Applicant 
and  
MARGO BERZACK First Respondent  
THE REGISTRAR OF DEEDS: CAPE TOWN Second Respondent 
THE CITY OF CAPE TOWN MUNICIPALITY Third Respondent 
 
APPLICANT’S WRITTEN ARGUMENT 
 
 
 
 2 
TABLE OF CONTENTS 
I INTRODUCTION 3 
II CENTRAL FACTS AND CENTRAL DISPUTE 4 
III SECTION 66 OF DEEDS REGISTRIES ACT 9 
(a) Introduction 9 
(b) Common law underpinning section 66 9 
(c) Interpretation and enforcement of section 66 16 
IV REQUIREMENTS FOR PRAEDIAL SERVITUDES 24 
V SUBVERSION OF LOCAL LAND USE ORDINANCE 32 
VI CLAUSE P(b)(i) OF SERVITUDE 34 
VII COUNTER-APPLICATION 36 
VIII JURISDICTION 38 
(a) Arguable points of law 38 
(b) Of general public importance 39 
(c) Interests of Justice 39 
IX CONCLUSION  40 
 
 3 
I        INTRODUCTION 
1 The applicant (“Huntrex 277”) was the first respondent in the Su-
preme Court of Appeal (“SCA”), in which the applicant in the mat-
ter, the present first respondent (“Mrs Berzack”), sought leave to 
appeal against a judgment delivered by a single Judge in the Court 
of first instance. By a majority of three to two the SCA granted 
leave to appeal and thereupon upheld the appeal. Huntrex 277 
wishes to appeal against that judgment. It maintains that the Minor-
ity Judgment was correct and that the Majority Judgment was in-
correct. In the premises Huntrex 277 seeks the leave of this Hon-
ourable Court to appeal to it against the Majority Judgment and or-
der of the SCA. 
2 The question raised in the proposed appeal is the following: 
Whether a servitude, which permitted the owner of a residential erf 
to undertake pleasurable gardening activities on approximately one 
quarter of a neighbouring residential erf and prescribed the materi-
al of which the garden fence might be constructed, (a) constituted a 
personal servitude of usus in favour of the owner of the first-
mentioned erf, so that, in terms of section 66 of the Deeds Regis-
tries Act 47 of 1937 (“section 66”) it was not capable of being law-
 4 
fully registered against the title deed of the last-mentioned erf inso-
far as it purported to extend beyond the lifetime of that owner, or 
whether, on the other hand, (b) was lawfully capable of being regis-
tered against the title deed of the last-mentioned erf (notionally the 
servient tenement) as a praedial servitude, thus in perpetuity bur-
dening it in favour of the first mentioned erf (notionally the domi-
nant tenement). 
II       CENTRAL FACTS AND CENTRAL DISPUTE 
3 Huntrex 277 is the owner of Erf 8478, Constantia (“the Huntrex 277 
property”). Mrs Berzack is the owner of the remainder of Erf 380, 
Constantia (“the Berzack property”).  
4 Mrs Berzack was previously the owner of Erf 380 Constantia (“the 
parent property”), on which was situated her house and an estab-
lished garden. Prior to 2 December 1982 she subdivided the parent 
property into two erven, namely the Berzack property (to the East) 
and the Huntrex 277 property (to the West). She wanted to retain 
the Berzack property, on which her house was situated, while sell-
ing the Huntrex 277 property. 
5 Mrs Berzack’s garden, which was physically demarcated along its 
Western perimeter by a wooden pole fence (which still exists), ex-
 5 
tended well onto that part of the parent property which, upon sub-
division, became the Huntrex 277 property. Mrs Berzack’s initial in-
tention had been to align the subdivision with the wooden pole 
fence, so that the whole of her garden would remain a part of the 
Berzack property.  
6 However, although none of the parties has been able to identify it, 
it is common cause that erven in the neighbourhood, including the 
parent property, were subject to a local land use ordinance 1, which 
imposed a minimum erf size of 4 000 m2 on erven 2. The parent 
property was too small to admit of the subdivision that Mrs Berzack 
sought: If it was so subdivided that the garden remained a part of 
the Berzack property, the Huntrex 277 property would be signifi-
cantly smaller than 4 000 m2, so that such subdivision was pro-
scribed by the ordinance. 
7 Accordingly, the subdivision of the parent property was effected in 
accordance with the ordinance. The Berzack property was 4320 m2 
 
1  This was likely a regulation in relation to a town planning scheme under section 35(1) and (2) read 
with section 60(e) and item 10 in the Second Schedule of Cape Ordinance 33 of 1934. 
2  Record Vol 1 p 77 (Answering affidavit para 28). 
 6 
in extent and the Huntrex 277 property was 4013 m2 in extent 3. 
8 It follows that after the subdivision a significant portion of Mrs Ber-
zack’s established garden lay within the Huntrex 277 property. In 
order to overcome this impact of the land use ordinance, Mrs Ber-
zack conceived a plan, which she expressed as follows 4: 
“As appears from annexure ‘MB-4’, an unavoidable conse-
quence of the subdivision was that a significant portion of my 
existing (private) garden fell within the Huntrex property. I 
wished to protect, for my benefit and for the successors in title 
to the Berzack property the exclusive right to use, access and 
tend that portion of the garden by creating a servitude over the 
Huntrex 277 property at the same time as selling and transfer-
ring it.”  
9 On 2 December 1982 Mrs Berzack sold the Huntrex 277 property 
to Mr A G Wellens. Her abovementioned plan was implemented in 
the deed of sale concluded between them, which provided for the 
introduction of the servitude now in issue as clause P of the deed 
of transfer (“the servitude”) 5. The servitude is quoted both in the 
 
3  Record Vol 1 p 30 (Founding Affidavit annexure “A”: title deed). 
4  Record Vol 1 p 78 (Answering Affidavit para 31). 
5  Record Vol 1 p 37 and 40 ( Founding Affidavit annexures A and B: title deed and diagram ). 
 7 
Majority Judgment and in the Minority Judgment 6, and is not here 
repeated. We simply wish to emphasise, in the light of the Minority 
judgment, that the servitude was imposed by Mrs Berzack “in fa-
vour of himself [sic] and his [sic] successors in title as owner of [the 
Berzack property]” but not in favour of the Berzack property itself. 
10 The right to “use, access and tend” the portion of the existing gar-
den on the Huntrex 277 property, which Mrs Berzack wished to se-
cure, was embodied in clauses P(b)(ii) and (iii) of the servitude. 
The actual rights conferred are (in clause P(b)(ii)) “the right to 
plant, control, care for and renew the existing garden” and (in 
clause P(b)(iii)) “full rights of access to such servitude area”. 
Clause P(b)(i) will be addressed below. In the light of the Majority 
Judgment it may be noted that the right to harvest produce from 
the garden was not expressly reserved. 
11 It is important to appreciate that the servitude, which is 20 metres 
wide, covers approximately one third (by estimation) of the surface 
area of the Huntrex 277 property 7. Because the wooden pole 
fence which demarcates the outer perimeter of the garden does not 
 
6  Record Vol 3 p 238 – 239 para [9] (Majority Judgment); p 253 para [37] (Minority Judgment). 
7  Record Vol 1 p 57 (Founding Affidavit annexure “E”), 
 8 
follow the servitude boundary, but runs diagonally across the servi-
tude area, the whole of the servitude area is not used as Mrs Ber-
zack’s garden. A visual examination of the relevant diagram re-
veals that approximately one quarter of the surface area of the 
Huntrex 277 property is actually used for the garden. 
12 Mrs Berzack has stated that it was her and Mr Wellens’s common 
understanding that the servitude would enure to any subsequent 
owners of the Berzack property 8, and this much has been con-
firmed by Mr Wellens 9. 
13 Huntrex 277 submits that, objectively assessed with reference to its 
stated content, the servitude was a personal servitude of usus in 
favour of Mrs Berzack personally, so that it was barred by section 
66 of the Deeds Registries Act from being lawfully registered as a 
praedial servitude, which would have had the effect of extending it 
beyond her lifetime. Mrs Berzack, on the other hand, submits that it 
was lawfully capable of being registered as a praedial servitude in 
perpetuity. That is, and has from the outset been, the central dis-
pute between Huntrex 277 and Mrs Berzack. 
 
8  Record Vol 1 p 79 (Answering Affidavit, para 32.6). 
9  Record Vol 2 p 126 (Affidavit of Mr Wellens, para 6). 
 9 
III      SECTION 66 OF THE DEEDS REGISTRIES ACT 47 OF 1937  
(a)     Introduction 
14 Section 3(1)(o) of the Deeds Registries Act provides that the Reg-
istrar of Deeds shall “register any servitude, whether personal or 
praedial, . . .”. There is, accordingly, no objection to the registration 
per se of personal servitudes. However, section 66 qualifies sec-
tion 3(1)(o) as follows: 
“No personal servitude of usufruct, usus or habitatio purporting 
to extend beyond the lifetime of the person in whose favour it is 
created shall be registered, nor may a transfer or cession of 
such personal servitude to any person other than the owner of 
the land encumbered thereby, be registered.” 
15 Huntrex 277 relies not only on the literal interpretation and en-
forcement of section 66, but also on the common law which under-
pins it.  
(b)     Common law underpinning section 66 
16 Ownership confers certain entitlements upon the owner of a thing, 
such as (a) the entitlement to use the thing (ius utendi); (b) the enti-
tlement to draw natural fruits (fructus naturales) and civil fruits 
(fructus civiles) from the thing (ius fruendi); and (c) the entitlement 
 10 
to possess the thing (ius possidendi) 10. We regard these as the 
“central entitlements” of ownership. There are other entitlements, 
such as (d) the entitlement to consume and destroy the thing (ius 
abutendi); (e) the entitlement to dispose of the thing (ius disponen-
di); the entitlement to claim the thing from an unlawful occupier (ius 
vindicandi); and the entitlement of resist any unlawful invasion (ius 
negandi) 11 but they do not arise for consideration in the present 
matter. 
17 Ownership has been described as a “mother right”, which is unlim-
ited in duration. It has a residuary character, which implies that no 
matter how many central entitlements the owner disposes of, he or 
she retains a reversionary right to them 12. There are, of course, 
numerous ways in which the central entitlements of ownership may 
be curtailed or lost. They may be curtailed by the owner himself or 
herself, for example, when the property is let, or is hypothecated, 
or is given in pledge. The entitlements of ownership may also be 
curtailed or lost independently of the will of the owner, for example, 
 
10  Silberberg & Schoeman The Law of Property (6th Ed) at 104 – 105; Van der Merwe Sakereg (2nd 
Ed) at 173. 
11  Ibid 
12  Silberberg & Schoeman The Law of Property (6th Ed) at 105 - 107; Van der Merwe Sakereg (2nd 
Ed) at 174 – 176. 
 11 
by the law of neighbours or by the law of expropriation or by nu-
merous legislative provisions which broadly fall within the scope of 
public law, such as, most commonly, town planning and building 
regulations (and the approvals required thereunder) 13. In this doc-
ument we focus solely on the curtailment or loss of the central enti-
tlements of ownership occasioned by servitudes. 
18 A servitude over property is a limited real right in terms of which the 
owner forfeits the full exercise of the central entitlements of owner-
ship in favour of a servitude-holder, either by (positively) conferring 
upon the servitude-holder a right to use or enjoy his property in a 
defined manner or by (negatively) conferring upon the servitude-
holder the right to restrain the owner from using or enjoying his 
property in a defined manner 14.  
19 An essential characteristic of praedial servitudes is that they are 
conferred in favour of a dominant tenement over a neighbouring or 
proximate servient tenement. Given the permanence of the tene-
ments, praedial servitudes constitute a perpetual burden upon the 
 
13  See generally, Silberberg & Schoeman The Law of Property (6th Ed) at 107 – 150; Van der Merwe 
Sakereg (2nd Ed) at 176 – 213. 
14  Silberberg & Schoeman The Law of Property (6th Ed) at 371 – 372; Van der Merwe Sakereg (2nd 
Ed) at 458 – 459, 463 (iv) 
 12 
servient tenement, well beyond the lifetime of the owner of the 
dominant tenement at the time that they were first granted 15. How-
ever, not one of the traditionally recognised praedial servitudes, 
whether they are rural 16 or urban 17, to a significant degree divest 
the servient owner of the central entitlements of ownership. They 
merely curtail the exercise of those entitlements, to the extent that 
it is necessary to accommodate the exercise by the dominant own-
er of his or her rights. The residuary character of ownership means 
that the servient owner may exercise all the central entitlements of 
ownership to the extent that they have not been so curtailed in fa-
vour of the dominant owner 18. Given this minimal curtailment of the 
central entitlements of the servient owner, it follows that in practice 
the traditionally recognised praedial servitudes leave intact the 
great preponderance of the servient owner’s central entitlements. 
 
15  Silberberg & Schoeman The Law of Property (6th Ed) at 372 – 373; Van der Merwe Sakereg (2nd 
Ed) at 467. 
16  As to rural praedial servitudes see Silberberg & Schoeman The Law of Property (6h Ed) at 377 – 
378; Van der Merwe Sakereg (2nd Ed) at 481 – 497. 
17  As to urban praedial servitudes see Silberberg & Schoeman The Law of Property (6h Ed) at 378 – 
379; Van der Merwe Sakereg (2nd Ed) at 497 – 501. Voet 8.2 (Gane’s Translation Vol 2 pp 438 – 
460 describes a plethora of urban praedial servitudes, which appear to be variations on a few cen-
tral themes. More recently, registered restrictive conditions governing the use of land or the erec-
tion of structures in townships have been recognised in South Africa as urban praedial servitudes, 
as to which see Silberbeg & Schoeman The Law of Property (5th Ed) at 343 – 356; Van der Merwe 
Sakereg (2nd Ed) at 501 – 505. 
18  Silberberg & Schoeman The Law of Property (6th Ed) at 379 para 14.3.3; Van der Merwe Sakereg 
(2nd Ed) at 464 – 465 (para i). 
 13 
In other words, the fact that praedial servitudes are of indefinite du-
ration ordinarily does not impact significantly on the servient own-
er’s enjoyment of the ownership of his or her land.  
20 On the other hand, the personal servitudes of usufruct 19, usus 20 
and habitatio 21 in respect of land are entirely different. They are 
conferred for the enjoyment of a person, and they do, to a greater 
or lesser extent, substantially divest the owner of the subject land 
of the central entitlements of ownership, by vesting them in the 
beneficiary under servitude. In the case of usufruct the central enti-
tlements are almost completely forfeit. In the case of usus and hab-
itatio they are forfeit to a somewhat lesser extent, but still to a de-
gree that leaves the subject property substantially useless in the 
hands of its owner. It is for this reason that at common law the du-
ration of the right conferred by a personal servitude was confined 
to the lifetime of the beneficiary of the right, ie, so that the right per-
 
19  LAWSA Vol 24 (2nd Ed) at para 581. See also Van der Merwe Sakereg (2nd Ed ) at 508; Silberberg 
& Schoeman The Law of Property (6th Ed) at 383, para 14.4.2. 
20  LAWSA Vol 24 (2nd Ed) at para 600. See also Van der Merwe Sakereg (2nd Ed ) at 521; Silberberg 
& Schoeman The Law of Property (6th Ed) at 337, para 14.4.3. 
21  LAWSA Vol 24 (2nd Ed) at para 600. See also Van der Merwe Sakereg (2nd Ed ) at 523; Silberberg 
& Schoeman The Law of Property (6h Ed) at 387, para 14.4.4 
 14 
ished with the beneficiary 22. The common law discountenanced 
the possibility that the ownership of land might in perpetuity be di-
vorced from the central entitlements of ownership, as is evidenced, 
for example, by the presumption against servitudes. 
21 Thus, a significant difference between a praedial servitude and a 
personal servitude is that the intensity of the burden imposed by 
the latter is much greater than the intensity of the burden imposed 
by the former. The following has been said in this regard 23: 
“If one considers the duration of a praedial servitude, this kind 
of servitude imposes a significant burden on the servient tene-
ment because the burden is in principle perpetual. However, if 
one considers the use and enjoyment entitlements of the servi-
ent owner, a personal servitudes (like usufruct) places a more 
intense burden on the servient owner over a relatively shorter 
period of time than a praedial servitude (like a right of way or a 
grazing right) which places a burden of comparable lower inten-
sity on the servient owner perpetually.” 
22 If personal servitudes were permitted to be of indefinite duration, 
 
22  Silberberg & Schoeman The Law of Property (6h Ed) at 372 – 373 footnote 17; Willoughby’s Con-
solidated Co Ltd v Copthall Stores Ltd 1913 AD 267 at 282 (“From the very nature of a personal 
servitude, the right which it confers is inseparably attached to the beneficiary. Res servit personae. 
He cannot transmit it to his heirs, nor can he, alienate it; when he dies it perishes with him. (Voet, 
8, 1, 4: Louw v Van der Post, etc.)”); See also Van der Merwe Sakereg (2nd Ed ) at 506, footnote 
351. 
 15 
like praedial servitudes, the beneficiary of the right, having become 
entitled in perpetuity to exercise the central entitlements of owner-
ship in respect of the subject property, would become the de facto 
owner of the subject property, while the denuded ownership of the 
owner of the subject property would correspondingly reduce him or 
her to a purely nominal or token owner of the subject property.  
23 It is respectfully submitted that the underpinning policy of section 
66 of the Act is to avoid the above outcome. This it expressly 
achieves, in relation to usufruct, usus and habitatio, by providing 
that those servitudes shall not “extend beyond the lifetime of the 
person in whose favour it is created”, or, in other words, that they 
are indeed personal servitudes in accordance with the common law 
restriction on their duration. We submit that the underpinning policy 
of the common law would preclude the registration as a praedial 
servitude of any personal servitude which approximates in restric-
tive effect to the personal servitudes mentioned above.  
24 Mrs Berzack indisputably relies on the servitude to divest the own-
er of the Huntrex 277 property in perpetuity of the central entitle-
ments of ownership in respect of about one quarter of the property, 
 
23  Silberbeg & Schoeman The Law of Property (6th Ed) at 373 
 16 
and to confer those entitlements in perpetuity upon the owners 
from time to time of the Berzack property. She is precluded from 
doing so not only by section 66, to which we now turn, but also by 
the common law that underpins it.  
(c)     Interpretation and enforcement of section 66 
25 The interpretation and enforcement of section 66 requires that 
meaning must be given to its language. The Deeds Registries Act 
does not define usufruct, usus or habitatio. One must accordingly 
turn to the common law, where those meanings are well-
established. Only the definition of usus is presently relevant. 
26 The Minority Judgment quoted the definition of usus as framed by 
Van der Walt 24. There are several other definitions of usus which 
correspond with Van der Walt’s definition 25. All of them have in 
common that usus substantially divests the owner of property of, 
and confers upon the beneficiary of the servitude, his or her ius 
utendi, his or her jus possidendi and (to a greater or lesser degree) 
his or her jus fruendi. A vital distinction between usus and usufruct 
 
24  Record Vol 3 p 260 para [54] Minority Judgment; Van der Walt The Law of Servitudes (Juta 1st Ed) 
at 488. 
 17 
is that in the case of usus the beneficiary may take the fruit, vege-
tables and other produce of the land for his or her own daily needs 
and the needs of his or her household, but that the rest of the pro-
duce belongs to the owner who may enter the land in order to 
gather the produce. In other words, usus curtails the servient own-
er’s ius fruendi to a lesser degree than usufruct, which allows the 
beneficiary to harvest all the produce of the land. In the present in-
stance this distinction is of little import, because the servitude area 
on the Huntrex 277 property is not and never was used for the cul-
tivation of produce. 
27 The Minority Judgment, having drawn attention to certain limita-
tions imposed by law upon the freedom of contracting parties to 
create servitudes by agreement between themselves, addressed 
the common law presumption that land is free of a servitude, and 
that in case of doubt the interpretation of the servitude which least 
encumbers the servient tenement will be adopted 26. The following 
 
25  Silberberg & Schoeman The Law of Property (6h Ed) at 387; Van der Merwe Sakereg (2nd Ed ) at 
506, footnote 521; LAWSA Vol 24 (2nd Ed) at para 600; Du Bois Wille’s Principles of South Africa 
Law (9th Ed) at 610.  
26  Record Vol 3 pp 355 -256 para [43] – [46] Minority Judgment and the authority there referred to: 
Willoughby’s Consolidated Co Ltd v Coptall Stores Ltd 1918 AD 1 at 16; Kruger v Joles Eien-
domme (Pty) Ltd 2009(3) SA 5 SCA) at para [8]; Jonordon Investments (Pty) Ltd v De Aar Drank-
winkel (Edms) Bpk 1969(2) SA 117 (C) at 126 A – B; Lorenz v Melle and Others 1978(3) SA 1044 
(T) at 1049 H. 
 18 
dictum was quoted with approval 27: 
“Where it is doubtful whether a servitudal burden placed on land 
was intended for the benefit of another property and therefore 
praedial and perpetual or for the benefit of a particular person 
and therefore, personal and limited in duration, the latter inter-
pretation must be adopted as being the one which places the 
lesser burden of the subject matter of the servitude.” 
28 The Minority Judgment thereupon proceeded to interpret the lan-
guage of the servitude, to establish whether it actually achieved the 
creation of a praedial servitude 28. On the basis inter alia of the fact 
that the servitude had been “imposed” by Mrs Berzack in favour of 
herself and her successors in title to the Berzack property, but not 
in favour of the Berzack property itself, as is strictly required for the 
creation of a praedial servitude, the Minority Judgment found that 
there was uncertainty about the true nature of the servitude. Thus, 
in the light of the presumption already mentioned above, the Minor-
ity Judgment found that as a matter of interpretation the servitude 
in casu was a personal servitude rather than a praedial servitude. 
We respectfully embrace this finding.  
 
27  Record Vol 3 pp 256 – 257 para 26] Jonordam Investment (Pty) Ltd v De Aar Drankwinkel (edms) 
Bpk 1969(2) SA 117 (C) at 126 A-B 
 19 
29 But even if the Minority Judgment should be wrong in this regard, 
and it is found that the creation of a praedial servitude was intend-
ed, the question remains whether Mrs Berzack and Mr Wellens 
were lawfully permitted by agreement between themselves to 
achieve that outcome. The Minority Judgment addressed also this 
question 29. It is undisputed that clauses P(b)(ii) and (iii) of the ser-
vitude were introduced because Mrs Berzack wanted to continue 
the pleasurable activity of gardening on the portion of the Huntrex 
277 property which she had sold 30. More particularly, her stated 
purpose was that she wanted to “use, access and tend that portion 
of the garden” that lay on the Huntrex 277 property 31.  
30 The Minority Judgment, in addressing this issue, expressed the fol-
lowing finding 32: 
“When Ms Berzack’s stated intention is matched with clauses 
P(b)(ii) and (III), there can be little doubt that she sought to, and 
did, create a right of use for herself. It seems clear to us that the 
servitude thus created falls squarely into the definition of the 
 
28  Record Vol 3 pp 258 – 260 paras [49] – [53] Minority judgment 
29  Record Vol 3 pp 54 – 56 paras [54] – [56] (Minority Judgment) 
30  Record Vol 1 p 22 ( Founding Affidavit, para 50 ), which has not been disputed: See Record Vol 1 p 
85 ( Answering Affidavit para 55 and 56 ). 
31  Record Vol 1 p 79 ( Answering Affidavit para 31 ). 
32  Record Vol 3 p 260 para 55 (Minority Judgment) 
 20 
personal servitude of usus.” 
31 We respectfully say that this finding is correct. Objectively regard-
ed, the right to use, access and tend a garden on the land of an-
other, thereby absolutely curtailing the ius utendi and ius possiden-
di of the owner, indisputably falls within all the definitions of the 
personal servitude of usus.  
32 Upon this point the Majority Judgment and the Minority Judgment 
diverge. According to the Majority Judgment the servitude does not 
fall within the definition of usus at all. As we understand it, the rea-
soning is that the servitude escapes that definition by virtue of the 
fact that in the case of usus the right is conferred upon the benefi-
ciary to take fruit, vegetables and other produce of the land for his 
or her own daily needs and the needs of his or her household, but 
that the rest of the produce belongs to the owner 33 (ie, allowing the 
owner of the subject property to exercise a qualified jus fruendi). 
The implication appears to be that the servitude could not have 
constituted usus unless it was expressly named as such, or unless 
it expressly preserved the ius fruendi of Huntrex 277 to the extent 
indicated, ie, by acknowledging the right of Huntrex 277 to take 
 21 
fruit, vegetables, etc, not used by Mrs Berzack. We respectfully re-
quest this Honourable Court to reject the above reasoning and im-
plication. The Majority Judgment disregarded the fact that Mrs Ber-
zack’s garden on the servitude area was not being used to cultivate 
anything which might be harvested. On the contrary, the available 
photographic evidence shows that a well-established pleasure gar-
den, with shrubs, flower beds and shading trees, existed on the 
servitude area 34. But even if there were fruit trees on the servitude 
area from which Huntrex 277 might harvest fruit unplucked by Mrs 
Berzack, the right to do so would flow naturally from the existence 
per se of the servitude of usus. It would not be necessary expressly 
to reserve that right to Huntrex 277 in order to establish the servi-
tude of usus. And the fact remains the curtailment of Huntrex 277’s 
ius utendi and ius possidendi in relation to the servitude area (re-
gardless of the right to take unused produce) were absolute. We 
therefore respectfully challenge the view of the Majority Judgment 
that, absent any provision which preserves the right of Huntrex 277 
to take produce not used by Mrs Berzack, the servitude “has all the 
hallmarks of a praedial servitude, not a personal servitude of usus”. 
 
33  Record Vol 3 p 249 – 250 paras [28] and [29] (Majority Judgment) 
 22 
We accept that there is no numerus clausus of servitudes, and that 
they may be incrementally expanded. But what the Majority Judg-
ment has done is not an incremental expansion. If the above pro-
nouncement of the Majority Judgment stands it may well serve as 
authority for the broader proposition that a servitude, which abso-
lutely curtails the ius utendi and the ius possidendi of the owner of 
a servient tenement, might property be registered as a praedial 
servitude in favour of a dominant tenement, thus extending the 
burden imposed dramatically beyond the very benign restrictions 
imposed by the presently recognised traditional praedial servi-
tudes.  
33 In the light of its finding that the servitude falls squarely into the 
definition of the personal servitude of usus, the Minority Judgment 
expressed the following conclusion 35: 
“As s. 66 of the Deeds Registries Act prohibits the registration 
of a personal servitude of usus that purports to extend beyond 
the lifetime of the person who created it, Ms Berzack was not 
legally capable of ‘imposing’ the servitude on the purchaser of 
her property in favour of herself and her successors in title. That 
 
34  Record Vol 2 pp103 – 109 (Answering Affidavit Annexures “MB6” to “MB12”) 
35  Record Vol 3 p 261 para [56]  
 23 
has the result that, irrespective of what the servitude says or 
what Ms Berzack intended, the servitude expires on her death. 
The import and effect of s. 66 of the Deeds Registries Act can-
not be ignored. It reflects a legislative purpose to bolster the 
common law impediments to the extension of perpetual re-
strictions on the ownership of property. Its effect is that once a 
servitude bears the hallmarks of a personal servitude, it pre-
cludes registration.” 
34 We respectfully say that, if this Honourable Court should uphold 
the finding that the servitude was indeed a personal servitude of 
usus, it should also uphold the above quoted conclusion of the Mi-
nority Judgment.  
35 We have already pointed out that the Minority Judgment, apply-
ing the presumption against servitudal burdens, found that as a 
matter of interpretation the servitude was a personal servitude ra-
ther than a praedial servitude. This Honourable Court may none-
theless find that it was intended to be, and was, registered as a 
praedial servitude. However, insofar as section 66 precluded the 
registration of a servitude of usus as a praedial servitude, the 
mere fact that the servitude was actually registered as a praedial 
 24 
servitude did not in fact convert it into a praedial servitude 36. It 
follows in principle that even if Mrs Berzack and Mr Wellens in their 
deed of sale envisaged the creation of a praedial servitude, the 
outcome was not lawfully capable of being implemented 37. 
IV      REQUIREMENTS FOR PRAEDIAL SERVITUDES 
36 We have hitherto addressed the question whether the servitude ob-
jectively fell within the ambit of usus, as it is used in section 66, in 
which event it was struck by the statutory prohibition against regis-
tration in that section. There is an alternative approach, namely, to 
enquire whether the servitude objectively complied with criteria for 
the creation of a praedial servitude. That was an alternative ap-
proach adopted also in the Minority Judgment 38. 
37 The objective requirements for a valid praedial servitude are (a) vi-
cinitas, (b) perpetual cause, (c) utilitas, (d) passivity and (e) indivis-
ibility 39. The Minority Judgment focused on the requirements of 
perpetual cause and utility, and found that neither had been satis-
 
36  Lorentz v Melle and Others 1978(3) SA 1044 (T) at 1049 G  
37  Eg Hubbard v Cool Ideas 1186 CC 2013(5) SA 112 (SCA) at para [10] ff and authority there cited. 
38  Record Vol 3 pp 262 – 267 paras [58] – [68] (Minority Judgment) 
 25 
fied. We respectfully embrace the views of the Minority Judgment 
in this regard 40, and merely wish to highlight certain aspects there-
of: 
37.1 The requirement of utilitas entails that a praedial servitude 
may not be conferred to serve the personal pleasure or ca-
price of the dominant owner, although there can be no objec-
tion if the dominant owner coincidentally derives pleasure 
from exercising a praedial servitude which is otherwise 
properly of utility to the dominant tenement 41. The origin of 
this requirement is Digest 8.1.8, which is as follows 42: 
“A servitude cannot be created to allow us to gather fruit or 
to stroll or picnic on another’s land” 
37.2 In the present matter the Majority Judgment expressly found 
that 43: 
 
39  LAWSA Vol 24 (2nd Ed) at para 546; Van der Merwe Sakereg (2nd Ed ) at 469 -471 (para (ii)) ex-
presses the requirements somewhat differently, insofar as he regards perpetual cause as implicit in 
the requirement of utilitas; Du Bois Wille’s Principles of South Africa Law (9th Ed) at 593 – 596.  
40  Including the authority cited: Briers v Wilson and Others 1952(3) SA 423 © at 433 H – 434 F; 
Bisschop v Stafford 1974(3) SA 1 (A) at 11 F – 12 A; Hollman and Another v Estate Latre 1970(3) 
SA 638 (A) at 644 F - 45 B 
41  LAWSA Vol 24 (2nd Ed) at para 549; Van der Merwe Sakereg (2nd Ed ) at 470 – 471; Du Bois 
Wille’s Principles of South Africa Law (9th Ed) at 593 – 596;  
42  Watson’s translation Vol 1. See also Voet 8,1.1 Gane’s Translation Vol 2 p432. 
43  Record Vol 3 p 247 para [24] Majority Judgment. 
 26 
“[t]he right to garden is reserved on the servient land and it 
enures in favour of the Berzack property, serving the pur-
suit of Ms Berzack’s personal pleasure or caprice” 44 
37.3 We respectfully say that the aforesaid express finding plainly 
entailed that the utilitas requirement had not been satisfied in 
respect of the servitude, which ought to have impelled the 
Majority Judgment to the conclusion that the servitude did not 
comply with all the requirements for a praedial servitude, and 
ought therefore not to be recognised as such. 
37.4 The Majority Judgment stated that “the modern praedial ser-
vitude of view is similar in substance to the garden servitude 
[contended for by Mrs Berzack] 45”. Elsewhere the following 
was stated 46: 
“An obvious concomitant of the garden servitude is that the 
servient tenement entitles the dominant tenement to view 
the grounds. The servitudal rights created indirectly serve 
as a guarantee that no structure can be constructed on the 
grounds designated as the garden area. In this fashion, the 
dominant tenement’s poolside entertainment area will al-
 
44  Record Vol 3 p 247 para [24] Majority Judgment 
45  Record Vol 3 p 247 para [24] Majority Judgment 
46  Record Vol 3 p 250 – 251 para [32] Majority Judgment 
 27 
ways be an area with a view as it is and will always be ad-
jacent to a section of vacant land. That a view adds utilitas 
and enhances the value of residential property is incontro-
vertible”. 
37.5 The Minority Judgment controverted the above dicta 47, and 
we respectfully embrace its views 48. We merely wish to add 
that, insofar as the Majority Judgment invokes the ad-
vantages of a view and/or the restriction of building in the 
garden area, it appears to invoke the rights historically con-
ferred by urban praedial servitudes such as the servitus ne 
luminibus officiatur, or the servitus ne prospectui officiatur 
(prospectus), or the servitus luminis immitendi, or the servitus 
altius non tollendi 49 These are negative urban praedial servi-
tudes, because they enable the dominant owner to forbid cer-
tain things from occurring on the servient tenement. No such 
negative urban praedial servitude is registered over the Hunt-
rex 277 property. We therefore respectfully submit that none 
 
47  Record Vol 3 pp 264 -265 paras [63] – [64] Minority Judgment 
48  Including the authority cited: Paola v Jeeva NO and Others 2004(1) SA 396 (SCA); Clark v Faraday 
2004(4) SA 564 (C); Muller NO and Others v City of Cape Town and Another 2006(5) SA 415 (C); 
True Motives 84 (Pty) Ltd v Nadhi (Ethikwini Municipalty as Amicus Curiae) 2009(4) SA 153 
(SCA).;  
49  Silberberg & Schoeman The Law of Property (6h Ed) at 378 para (a); Van der Merwe Sakereg (2nd 
Ed ) at 498 – 499; LAWSA Vol 24 (2nd Ed) at para 572. 
 28 
of the rights conferred by them could properly be regarded as 
flowing by implication from the registered servitude in casu. 
The reason is that the servitude is a positive personal servi-
tude, affirmatively permitting Mrs Berzack to use, access and 
tend a garden, and is therefore utterly dissimilar to the nega-
tive urban praedial servitudes mentioned above. Moreover, 
the negative urban praedial servitudes mentioned above 
have largely been overtaken by the operation of public law, 
such as town planning and building regulations (and the ap-
provals required thereunder), and are nowadays rarely en-
countered 50. In relation to tenements where views are im-
portant the servitudes of prospectus and ne prospectui offici-
atur may still play a role, but they have to be registered as 
such and can only be imposed in respect of a place which 
lies lower than the point from which the view is enjoyed 51. 
There is no evidence that this is true of the Berzack property 
vis-à-vis the Huntrex 277 property. These issues do not have 
to be dealt with more fully in the proposed appeal, but we re-
spectfully suggest that they demonstrate that the Majority 
 
50  Van der Merwe Sakereg (2nd Ed ) at 499; LAWSA Vol 24 (2nd Ed) at para 576, 577 and 578. 
 29 
Judgment was incorrect in relying on the aforesaid negative 
urban praedial servitudes to give content to or to interpret the 
servitude. We respectfully say that to the extent that the Ma-
jority Judgment did so it was mistaken. 
38 A focus of Mrs Berzack’s reasoning in defending the servitude as a 
praedial servitude is that her successors-in-title are likely to derive 
economic benefit from the gardening rights which the servitude 
confers, which benefit satisfies the utilitas requirement for praedial 
servitudes. That her successors-in-title will derive such benefit is 
self-evidently true. Every subsequent owner of property in favour of 
which personal rights have been registered as praedial servitudes 
will obviously, inevitably derive economic benefit from the perpetui-
ty of the personal entitlements so conferred — simply because 
such perpetuity is to a greater or lesser extent tantamount to the 
conferring of ownership.  
39 But Mrs Berzack’s reasoning is fallacious because it begs an af-
firmative answer to the antecedent question whether those rights 
may be lawfully conferred at all upon her successors-in-title. Sec-
tion 66 and the common law proclaim that they cannot.  
 
51  Kruger v Downer 1976(3) SA 172 (W) at 178 C – G. 
 30 
40 It is respectfully submitted that the provisions of section 66 define 
the boundary between praedial servitudes and the personal servi-
tudes of usufruct, usus or habitatio, beyond which any broadening 
or development of the utilitas requirement for praedial servitudes 
cannot be permitted to expand. If that were not so, section 66 
would be a dead letter, at any rate in relation to adjacent or proxi-
mate erven, because it would always be possible to register the 
aforesaid personal servitudes as praedial servitudes by the simple 
expedient of not naming the servitudes but simply expressing in a 
title deed the reservation of such personal entitlements as are de-
sired; and thereupon arguing, as Mrs Berzack does, that those en-
titlements are of utility to the dominant tenement — which they 
would inevitably be. This could lead to the potentially chaotic prolif-
eration of praedial servitudes which impose hitherto unheard of 
burdens upon servient tenements, concomitantly extinguishing the 
central entitlements of ownership of the servient owners. An ex-
treme example would be where all the rights of usufruct are re-
served in favour of a dominant tenement without actually naming 
the servitude as usufruct. The utility in that case for the dominant 
tenement — the increase in its value — would obviously be im-
mense, but nothing would remain of the dominant owner’s owner-
 31 
ship except a bare title. 
41 In the final analysis Mrs Berzack contends for a praedial servitude 
which did not feature among the older, traditional servitudes and 
which, we respectfully submit, should not be recognised in con-
temporary South African law, namely, a servitudal right which in 
perpetuity confers upon the dominant tenement (and in perpetuity 
divests the servient tenement of) the central entitlements of owner-
ship in respect of up to one quarter of the surface area of the servi-
ent tenement. 
42 It is necessary to draw attention to the judgment in Olive Marketing 
CC v Eden Crescent Share Block Ltd and Others  2021(2) SA 170 
(KZD). In that matter a servitude which was substantially a person-
al servitude of usus was registered as a praedial servitude over 
(apparently) a very large portion of the servient tenement (as a 
parking servitude for at least 250 vehicles). Reliance was placed in 
Olive Marketing (at para [69] footnote 41) on Body Corporate Sea-
scapes v Ford and Others 2009(1) SA 252 (SCA) as authority for 
the unqualified proposition that “a parking servitude is one of the 
praedial servitudes permitted under modern property law”.  
43 It is respectfully submitted, however, that the latter case was actu-
 32 
ally concerned with servitudes granted and registered specifically 
in terms of section 29 of the Sectional Titles Act 95 of 1986, and 
did not support the unqualified proposition made by the Court in Ol-
ive Marketing. It is respectfully submitted that Olive Marketing was 
incorrectly decided, because the Court in that matter was not re-
ferred to and did not consider the impact of section 66 of the Deeds 
Registries Act.  
V      SUBVERSION OF LOCAL LAND USE ORDINANCE 
44 It is common cause that the servitude was prompted by Mrs Ber-
zack’s wish to overcome the effect of a local land use ordinance, 
which imposed a minimum erf size of 4 000 m2 on erven in the 
neighbourhood. Such restrictions are imposed because a compe-
tent local authority has, as a matter of policy, established minimum 
criteria for the viability of residential erven in a particular neigh-
bourhood. They are public law restrictions, legislatively imposed 
upon property and cannot be waived unless all lot-holders agree 52. 
45 Mrs Berzack purported to sell the Huntrex 277 property while re-
taining for herself and her successors-in-title, in perpetuity, the cen-
 
52  LAWSA Vol 24 (2nd Ed) at para 578 
 33 
tral competences of ownership in relation to so much of her exist-
ing garden as lay upon the Huntrex 277 property. That meant the 
Berzack property was de facto substantially larger than its actually 
registered size. However, it also meant, conversely, that the Hunt-
rex 277 property was de facto substantially smaller than its actually 
registered size, and well under the prescribed minimum of 4 000 
m2. It follows that the servitude in effect subverted the local land 
use ordinance, and flouted its underlying policy, by creating the 
Huntrex 277 property as an erf which was actually much smaller 
than was regarded as viable in the area. Any official diagram of the 
Huntrex 277 property, purporting to show it as an erf with a mini-
mum extent of 4 000 m2, is substantially a fiction. 
46 Mrs Berzak has candidly admitted that the express purpose of the 
servitude was to circumvent the local land use ordinance, and thus 
to achieve the above outcome, which, we respectfully suggest, was 
an illegal purpose. If that is so, the contract between Mrs Berzak 
and Mr Wellens, to the extent that it was actuated by the aforesaid 
illegal purpose, was unenforceable and could not have culminated 
 34 
in the lawful registration of the servitude53. 
VI      CLAUSE P(b)(i) OF THE SERVITUDE 
47 Clause P(b)(i) of the servitude provides as follows: 
“No wall or fence of any description shall be erected on the ser-
vitude boundary except extension of existing type of fencing 
(wooden pole fencing).” 
48 It is respectfully submitted that clause P(b)(i) is not concerned with 
regulating the construction per se of barriers of any kind along the 
servitude boundary. That such construction may occur is admitted 
in the clause — provided only that it consists of “the existing type of 
fencing (wooden pole fencing)”. The exception, it is submitted, is 
the essence of the provision. If so, clause P(b)(i) is actually con-
cerned with preserving an aesthetic of which the existing wooden 
pole fencing was a component.  
49 Huntrex 277 assumed, without admitting 54, that clause P(b)(i) em-
bodied a negative urban praedial servitude which regulates the use 
of land or the erection of structures upon it (including building ma-
 
53  Bradford Christies Law of Contract (7th Ed) pp 446 – 448; Colonial Banking and Trust Co Ltd v Hill’s 
Trustee 1927 AD 488 at 495. 
54  Record  Vol 1 p 18 (Founding Affidavit para 40) 
 35 
terials to be used) and which is calculated to preserve the charac-
ter or aesthetic of an area 55. Relying on the aforesaid assumption, 
Mrs Berzack has argued that because clause P(b)(i) appears with 
clauses P(b)(ii) and (iii) in a single clause, the latter, as a result of 
the unitary nature of servitudes, necessarily partake of the nature 
of the former, so that they must all be construed collectively as a 
single praedial servitude. Thus, the argument appears to go, even 
if clauses P(b)(ii) and (iii) in substance constitute a personal servi-
tude, the clauses must all be collectively interpreted, as a whole, as 
being praedial, because that is the nature of clause P(b)(i).  
50 However, it is entirely possible for rights, which are typically or tra-
ditionally regarded as praedial, to be reserved as personal servi-
tudes. These are so-called servitutes irregulares 56. Clauses P(b)(ii) 
and (iii) are obviously the main operative provisions of the servi-
tude. Clause P(b)(i) was calculated merely to accommodate Mrs 
Berzack’s personal preference about the appearance of the fence 
around the garden. Thus, if effect is to be given to the unitary na-
ture of servitudes, the interpretation of the servitude should pro-
 
55  LAWSA Vol 24 (2nd Ed) at paras 576 to 578. 
56  Van der Merwe Sakereg (2nd Ed ) at 507 (para(i)). 
 36 
ceed on the basis that clause P(b)(i) partakes of the nature of 
P(b)(ii) and (iii), and not vice versa, so that the clauses must all be 
collectively interpreted, as a whole, as being personal. 
51 If, however, it were to be found that clause P(b)(i) embodied a 
negative urban praedial servitude calculated to preserve the char-
acter or aesthetic of the area, it is respectfully submitted that that 
servitude is plainly severable from the personal servitude of usus 
embodied in clauses P(b)(ii) and (iii) — despite the fact that they 
appear in the same principal clause — so that only the former was 
capable of registration as praedial, while the latter were not. We 
respectfully say that this must be so because the right to preserve 
the appearance of a garden fence has no or very scant impact on 
the diminution of the servient owner’s ius utendi and ius possiden-
di, which is plainly the main, overall impact of the servitude. It is re-
spectfully submitted that the reasoning in Bondev Midrand (Pty) Ltd 
v Puling and Another and a Similar Case 2017(6) SA 373 (SCA) at 
paras [19] and [20] is mutatis mutandis applicable. 
VII    COUNTER-APPLICATION 
52 Mrs Berzack’s counter-application was noted in the SCA, but was 
not decided either in the Majority Judgment or the Minority Judg-
 37 
ment. 
53 For the reasons set out above it is respectfully submitted that Mrs 
Berzack’s counter-application for declaratory relief was correctly 
dismissed by the Court of first instance 57. 
54 Insofar as Mrs Berzack in the alternative sought rectification of the 
servitude, in accordance with a draft proposed in prayer 2 of her 
notice of counter-application 58, it is submitted, again for the rea-
sons set out above, that such rectification was not justified. Once it 
is plain that the prohibited outcome envisaged by Mrs Berzack and 
Mr Wellens in their deed of sale was not lawfully capable of being 
implemented, it is an exercise in futility to rectify the servitude so as 
to articulate the forbidden outcome more precisely. 
55 Insofar as Mrs Berzack in the further alternative sought an order 
declaring that she had by acquisitive prescription acquired the 
praedial servitude contended for, it is submitted that, for the rea-
sons set out in its judgment, the Court of first instance correctly re-
 
57  Record Vol 2 p 160 (Order para 11). 
58  Record Vol 2 pp 121 – 122 (Notice of Counter-Application, para 2). 
 38 
fused to grant the order sought 59. 
VIII    JURISDICTION 
56 This Honourable Court’s jurisdiction is invoked under section 
167(3)(b)(ii) of the Constitution because the matter concerns an 
arguable point of law of general public importance which ought to 
be considered by this Honourable Court. 
(a)     Arguable points of law 
57 As set out by this Honourable Court in Paulsen v Slip Knot Invest-
ments 777 (Pty) Ltd 2015(3) SA 479 (CC), this matter is arguable 
as the SCA “expressed itself by a narrow majority” and the minority 
is “quite forceful” 60. The issues of law to be decided on appeal are 
set out above. There is no factual dispute that requires this Court’s 
determination. 
58 In addition, the matter does not entail the misapplication of settled 
law. The Majority Judgment in the SCA misstated the principles 
applicable when interpreting and enforcing servitude. This mis-
statement now reflects the law. 
 
59  Record Vol 2 pp 156 – 157 (Judgment paras 18 – 20). 
60  Paulsen v Slip Knot Investments 777 (Pty) Ltd 2015(3) SA 479 (CC) at 492, para [23](a) – (b).  
 39 
(b)     Of general public importance 
59 The public has an interest in the correct approach to the interpreta-
tion and enforcement of servitudes. As such, this matter “trans-
cend[s] the narrow interests of the litigants and implicate[s] the in-
terests of a significant part of the general public”.61  
60 The matter is of importance as it concerns perpetual burdens that 
may be imposed upon immovable property by agreement between 
private parties, without regard to the dictates of public policy that 
land should not be excessively so burdened. 
(c)     Interests of justice 
61 As to the interests of justice, the relevant factor in this case is 
whether there are reasonable prospects of success on appeal. As 
demonstrated above, if the Minority Judgment’s approach to inter-
pretation and enforcement of servitudes is upheld, there are rea-
sonable prospects of the servitude in casu being regarded as a 
personal servitude of usus which may not be registered insofar as 
it extends beyond Mrs Berzack’s lifetime. 
62 It is respectfully submitted, in the premises, that Huntrex 277 has 
 40 
reasonable prospects of success in an appeal against the SCA’s 
judgment and orders. 
IX     CONCLUSION 
63 In the premises we respectfully request this Honourable Court to 
grant Huntrex 277 leave to appeal, as sought in its Notice of Appli-
cation in this matter, and that a costs award, if any, shall include 
the costs of two counsel. 
64 It is possible that this Honourable Court, having granted leave to 
appeal, may decide forthwith to determine the appeal itself. In that 
event we respectfully request that this written argument shall be 
used also in relation to the merits of the appeal, and that, if it suc-
ceeds, the order proposed in the Minority Judgment 62 shall be 
substituted for the order of the SCA.  
 
S P ROSENBERG SC 
T R TYLER 
Chambers, Cape Town 
27 November 2023 
 
61  Paulsen v Slip Knot Investments 777 (Pty) Ltd 2015(3) SA 479 (CC) at 494 para [26]. 
62  Record Vol 3 pp 268 – 269 para [72] Minority Judgment
synopsis:
Servitude — whether praedial or personal — approach to interpretation of servitude — whether servitude compliant with principles of property law

Praedial servitude — requirements of perpetuity and utility — whether utility satisfied by recreational use
Personal servitude — whether servitude constituting personal servitude of usus

Section 66 of Deed Registries Act 47 of 1937 — prohibiting registration of certain personal servitudes extending beyond the lifetime of the holder

Common law of servitudes — whether same should be developed or modernised by way of new statutory regime
Servitude — exercise required to be civiliter modo or reasonable
case_history: On application for leave to appeal from the Supreme Court of Appeal (hearing an appeal from the High Court of South Africa, Western Cape Division, Cape Town)
